name: Josh Hawley
tweet: The point of Barnes is that it reads 230c1 so broadly, to encompass decisions restricting access to third party material, that it swallows c2. No need for good faith. The second case, e-ventures, points this out and cites other courts that have taken the same misguided approach

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: Thanks @glennbeck for having me on today to talk about The Tyranny of Big Tech!

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: I also cite Justice Thomas’ thorough discussion of this redrafting by the courts - he cites and expands on the same line of cases I cite. You ignore all this

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: Except you get the case law wrong, while accusing me of misconstruing it. Both cases I cite on the Section 230 “good faith” provision illustrate the expansion of 230c1 to point of swallowing 230c2 and, in the case of the e-ventures case, explain the dangers of this move

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: And it’s a minor point, but I don’t say the Clayton Act “established” the FTC. I say “Wilson’s legislation ... gave the FTC enforcement authority” and “set up” the FTC - as indeed the Clayton Act empowers & defines FTC enforcement, which I argue helped normalize new corp order

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: You can disagree with this interpretation, but it’s not a “factual error.” And again, there’s a lot of scholarship on this that this reviewer either doesn’t know or ignores

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: For that reason, I conclude “the new FTC ... emerged as a project to regulate and normalize the corporate behemoths rather than dissolve them.” Here again, I am following progressive scholarship, particularly Martin Sklar, whom I cite

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: No. What I say is that the Supreme Court changed course in 1911 and limited the reach of federal antitrust law, a major about face that helped entrench corporate power. Wilson’s antitrust reforms didn’t change this outcome, but built on it & worked within it

tweet: In defense of these particular critics, they’re not historians or legal experts, they’re political commentators. That’s why engaging with the relevant historical literature and case law is so important. But thanks for reading!

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: To such a degree that c2, which contains the “good faith” takedown provision, basically becomes obsolete. I cite a couple of cases that trace this evolution. And I also cite Justice Thomas’ superb opinion in the Malwarebytes case that sets out the analysis - worth reading

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: As to Section 230, the @WIRED write up in particular takes me to task for allegedly getting the “good faith” provision wrong. But the reviewer doesn’t appear to have read or understood the relevant cases, which walk through the courts’ expansion of Section 230c1

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: Even though TR gave too much ground on monopoly, the TR-Wilson battle still matters because TR remained a defender of republican political economy. Wilson abandoned it. Efforts to rein in monopoly that aren’t linked to republican ideals won’t rescue self government

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: This new pro-corporate consensus. I take Theodore Roosevelt to task in the book for getting too comfortable w/ monopoly power himself. The corporate liberal consensus has informed our politics for decades. And no, FDR didn’t fundamentally alter the balance, as I mention in book

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: His antitrust reforms in fact ultimately helped cement the new corporate liberal consensus, w/ a big assist from the Supreme Court. Again, there’s an extensive secondary literature on this for those who care to read. And my argument is that both left AND right basically accepted

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: Wilson changed his rhetoric for a period in 1912, to try to contrast himself with Theodore Roosevelt. I talk about this in the book. But the conversion was mostly rhetorical, and fleeting. Wilson never became a Brandeisian

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: That’s lazy. Do the work. Take the time to understand the historical debate & engage with it. I stand by my analysis of Wilson’s writings and speeches over the decades of his career. The guy was comfortable with bigness & corporatism

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: I think these historians are largely correct, and I cite some of the extensive secondary literature in the book. My lefty critics, however, don’t engage with any of this historical debate or even seem aware of it. They claim that calling Wilson a corporatist is a factual “error”

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: There is some irony here. The fiercest critics of Wilson as a corporatist come from progressive historians, who have argued for decades now that Wilson was basically okay w/ monopoly power & corporate power more generally

name: Josh Hawley

tweet: Global economic integration now amped up and pursued for its own sake. Nat security, domestic needs, & liberalizing agenda diverge. Consequences for USA not so good. This system facilitated the “China shock,” which has had major security implications as well as economic ones

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: Uruguay Round marks significant departure. Industries & sectors formerly outside GATT rules now brought in. And the largely advisory dispute resolution process of GATT replaced w/ binding WTO process, complete w/ appellate body able to rely on its own decisions as precedent

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: This is why GATT left such large areas of economy & industry untouched, and allowed nations such wide latitude in domestic regulation. It was never trade liberalizing or integration just for the sake of integration

name: Josh Hawley
tweet: And my claim has been that in important respects, Bretton Woods, GATT placed the first in service to the second. Trade was meant to serve the end goals of national economic strength & domestic stability among member states
